learning_objectives: Identify the four principles of biomedical ethics (Beauchamp and Childress)
Understand how ethical frameworks apply to health informatics decisions
Distinguish between ethics, law, and professional codes of conduct
readings: Principles of Biomedical Ethics (Beauchamp and Childress) Ch. 1-3
AMIA Code of Professional and Ethical Conduct

lecture_notes.introduction: Every technology decision in healthcare has ethical dimensions. From who can access patient data to how AI algorithms are trained, health informatics professionals face ethical choices that affect patient lives.
key_concepts: Four principles: autonomy (patient self-determination), beneficence (do good), nonmaleficence (do no harm), justice (fair distribution)
Ethical theories: utilitarianism (greatest good), deontology (duty-based), virtue ethics (character)
Ethics vs. law: legal compliance is necessary but not sufficient for ethical practice
Professional codes: AMIA, AHIMA, HIMSS ethical guidelines for informatics
Ethical reasoning process: identify the ethical issue, gather facts, consider principles, evaluate options, decide, reflect
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis: a hospital IT team discovers a software bug that may have affected medication dosing calculations for 200 patients. Apply the four principles to determine the ethical course of action.

review.value: Respecting the patient's right to make informed decisions about their own healthcare and health data
Fair and equitable distribution of healthcare resources and burdens - relevant to algorithmic fairness and health equity

class_schedule.topic: Informed Consent and Patient Autonomy in the Digital Age
learning_objectives: Analyze how digital health technologies challenge traditional informed consent
Evaluate consent models for health data use in research and analytics
Understand patient rights under HIPAA and the Cures Act from an ethical perspective
readings: Rothstein MA. Ethical Issues in Big Data Health Research. JLME 2015

lecture_notes.introduction: Informed consent was designed for a pre-digital world. When a single blood draw generates genomic data reusable for decades, and EHR data fuels AI models, traditional consent frameworks are strained.
key_concepts: Traditional informed consent: disclosure, understanding, voluntariness, competence, authorization
Challenges in digital health: broad consent for data reuse, dynamic consent models, consent fatigue
Secondary use of health data: when does using EHR data for quality improvement require consent?
Patient data rights: access, amendment, accounting of disclosures, OpenNotes
Ethical tension: patient autonomy vs. societal benefit of health data research
lecture_notes.demonstration: Debate exercise: should hospitals be able to use de-identified EHR data for AI model training without individual patient consent? Arguments for and against.

review.value: One-time consent for future unspecified research uses of biospecimens and data - permitted under revised Common Rule but ethically debated
Technology-enabled consent model allowing patients to adjust their consent preferences over time for different data uses through a digital platform

learning_objectives: Distinguish between privacy, confidentiality, and security from an ethical standpoint
Analyze ethical challenges in de-identification and re-identification risks
Evaluate the ethics of health data monetization and commercial use
readings: Ohm P. Broken Promises of Privacy. UCLA Law Review 2010

lecture_notes.introduction: Health data privacy is not just a legal requirement. It is an ethical obligation rooted in the trust patients place in the healthcare system. When that trust is violated, patients withhold information, harming their own care.
key_concepts: Privacy: the right to control access to one's personal health information
Confidentiality: the obligation of those who receive health information to protect it
De-identification limits: re-identification risks in an era of big data and data linkage
Data monetization: ethical questions when health data generates commercial value
Surveillance and public health: when does the societal benefit of surveillance justify individual privacy intrusion?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study: a health data analytics company uses de-identified hospital data to build risk scores sold to life insurance companies. Analyze the ethical implications.

review.value: Research has shown that supposedly de-identified datasets can often be re-identified by linking with external data, challenging the assumption that de-identification eliminates privacy risk
When hospitals profit from selling patient data (even de-identified), patients who generated that data receive no benefit and often are unaware - raising justice concerns

class_schedule.topic: Ethics of Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare
learning_objectives: Identify ethical challenges specific to healthcare AI systems
Analyze algorithmic bias and its impact on health equity
Evaluate frameworks for responsible AI development in healthcare
readings: Char DS, et al. Implementing Machine Learning in Health Care. NEJM 2018
Obermeyer Z, et al. Dissecting Racial Bias in an Algorithm. Science 2019

lecture_notes.introduction: AI in healthcare presents novel ethical challenges: opaque algorithms making life-affecting decisions, training data reflecting historical biases, and the question of who is responsible when AI errs.
key_concepts: Algorithmic bias: how training data encodes historical health disparities into AI predictions
The Optum case: using healthcare costs as a proxy for need systematically disadvantaged Black patients
Transparency and explainability: can patients and clinicians understand why an AI made a recommendation?
Accountability: who is responsible when an AI diagnostic tool makes an error - developer, hospital, clinician?
Ethical AI frameworks: fairness, accountability, transparency, equity (FATE)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ethical analysis of the Optum algorithm case: identify the ethical violations using the four principles and propose corrections

review.value: Using seemingly neutral variables (like healthcare costs) that correlate with protected characteristics (race) as algorithmic inputs, perpetuating systemic discrimination
The degree to which an AI system's decision process can be understood by humans - ethically important because patients and clinicians deserve to understand decisions affecting health

class_schedule.topic: Health Equity and Social Justice in Informatics
learning_objectives: Analyze how health informatics systems can perpetuate or reduce health disparities
Evaluate the digital divide and its impact on health equity
Design informatics solutions with equity as a core requirement
readings: Veinot TC, et al. Good Intentions Are Not Enough: How Informatics Interventions Can Worsen Inequality. JAMIA 2018

lecture_notes.introduction: Health informatics can be a powerful force for equity or a hidden driver of disparity. Patient portals that require digital literacy, AI models trained on non-representative data, and telehealth that requires broadband all risk widening gaps.
key_concepts: Digital divide: access, literacy, and usability barriers disproportionately affecting marginalized populations
Inverse care law in informatics: those who need services most often benefit least from technology
Inclusive design: designing health IT for diverse users including disability, language, literacy levels
SDOH data collection: ethical considerations in screening for social needs
Equity metrics: measuring whether informatics interventions benefit all populations equally
lecture_notes.demonstration: Equity audit of a patient portal: evaluate usability for elderly patients, non-English speakers, patients with visual impairments, and patients without smartphones

review.value: Technology interventions often benefit the already-advantaged most (tech-savvy, educated, English-speaking) while the populations with greatest health needs face the most barriers
Designing technology for the full range of human diversity from the start, rather than retrofitting accessibility as an afterthought

class_schedule.topic: Professional Ethics and Moral Distress in Health IT
learning_objectives: Navigate ethical dilemmas faced by health informatics professionals
Develop a personal ethical framework for professional practice
readings: AMIA Code of Professional and Ethical Conduct
Goodman KW. Ethics, Medicine, and Information Technology. Cambridge University Press (selected chapters)

lecture_notes.introduction: Health informatics professionals face unique ethical dilemmas: pressure to deploy systems before they are safe, awareness of privacy violations, conflicts between organizational interests and patient welfare.
key_concepts: Professional obligations: duty to patients, employers, profession, and society
Moral distress: knowing the right action but being unable to take it due to organizational constraints
Whistleblowing: when and how to report safety concerns, legal protections
Conflicts of interest: vendor relationships, academic-industry ties, personal financial interests
Developing a personal ethical framework: values clarification and ethical decision-making practice
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ethical dilemma role-play: you discover that the EHR system you help manage has a defect that occasionally displays allergies from the wrong patient. Management wants to fix it quietly without disclosure. What do you do?

review.value: Psychological stress experienced when one knows the ethically correct action but institutional constraints prevent taking that action - common in health IT when safety concerns are deprioritized
Health informatics professionals have an ethical obligation to report patient safety risks in health IT systems, even when doing so is organizationally uncomfortable

questions.question: Apply all four principles of biomedical ethics to the following scenario: A hospital wants to use all patient EHR data to train an AI model that predicts sepsis, potentially saving lives. Patients were not specifically consented for AI research. Analyze the ethical considerations.
questions.answer: Autonomy: patients did not specifically consent to AI research use of their data. Even though data may be used for quality improvement under HIPAA, using it for AI model development may exceed patients' reasonable expectations. Respect for autonomy suggests at minimum transparency about data use. Beneficence: a sepsis prediction model could save many lives by enabling earlier intervention. The potential benefit to future patients is significant. Nonmaleficence: risks include: privacy violations if data is improperly handled, potential harm if the model encodes biases that disadvantage certain patient groups, and erosion of trust if patients learn their data was used without knowledge. Justice: was the training data representative of all patient populations? If the model performs better for some demographic groups than others, its deployment could worsen health disparities. Also, who benefits - if the hospital profits from commercializing the model, justice questions arise about benefit sharing with patients whose data made it possible.

rubric.key: Practical ethical considerations for informatics identified

questions.question: A researcher claims their dataset is 'fully de-identified' and therefore poses no privacy risk. Explain why this claim may be misleading and describe the ethical obligations that persist even with de-identified health data.
questions.answer: The claim is misleading because: (1) Re-identification is increasingly feasible through data linkage - studies have shown that combining de-identified health data with public records, social media, or other datasets can re-identify individuals. The 18 HIPAA identifiers represent a minimum, not a guarantee of anonymity. (2) Unique combinations of diagnoses, dates, and demographics can make individuals identifiable even without direct identifiers. (3) As data accumulates over time, re-identification risk increases. Ethical obligations that persist: (1) Minimize data collection to what is necessary (data minimization principle). (2) Use appropriate security safeguards even for de-identified data. (3) Contractually restrict downstream recipients from attempting re-identification. (4) Be transparent about how de-identified data will be used. (5) Consider the contextual integrity of data use - even if legally permissible, using health data in ways patients would not expect violates their reasonable privacy expectations.

rubric.key: Explains re-identification risks with specific examples
Identifies at least 3 persistent ethical obligations

questions.question: Describe the ethical failure in the Optum/UnitedHealth algorithm that Obermeyer et al. identified in their 2019 Science paper. What ethical principle was primarily violated, and what should have been done differently?
questions.answer: The Optum algorithm was designed to predict which patients would benefit from enrollment in a care management program. It used healthcare costs as a proxy for healthcare needs. Because Black patients historically had lower healthcare spending (due to systemic barriers to access, not lower health needs), the algorithm systematically assigned them lower risk scores than equally sick White patients. At any given risk score, Black patients were significantly sicker. The primary ethical principle violated was justice: the algorithm perpetuated and amplified existing racial disparities in healthcare access. The developers should have: (1) validated that their proxy variable (cost) accurately represented need across racial groups before deployment, (2) tested model performance for fairness across demographic groups, (3) used health outcomes (not costs) as the prediction target, (4) involved diverse stakeholders including community representatives in model design, (5) implemented ongoing monitoring for disparate impact after deployment.

rubric.key: Accurate description of the algorithmic failure

questions.question: You are a health informatics professional who discovers that your hospital's patient portal is effectively inaccessible to patients who speak only Spanish (the second most common language in your patient population). What ethical principles apply, and what actions should you take?
questions.answer: Ethical principles: (1) Justice: Spanish-speaking patients are denied equal access to their health information and portal features available to English-speaking patients. This is inequitable. (2) Autonomy: patients cannot exercise their right to access their health records and participate in their care if the portal is in a language they cannot read. (3) Beneficence: portal features (medication reminders, secure messaging, result review) improve health outcomes, but only for those who can use them. (4) Nonmaleficence: the inaccessible portal could cause harm if Spanish-speaking patients miss important results or appointment reminders. Actions: (1) Document the issue and present it to leadership with data on the affected patient population size. (2) Advocate for Spanish language support as a priority, not a nice-to-have. (3) Ensure compliance with Title VI of the Civil Rights Act (requires language access for federally funded entities). (4) If vendor support is delayed, implement interim solutions (multilingual navigation guides, bilingual staff outreach). (5) Track portal adoption rates by primary language as an equity metric.

rubric.key: Actionable steps beyond just raising the issue
Awareness of legal and equity dimensions

major_assignments.title: Health Informatics Ethics Case Analysis Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Analyze three real-world health informatics ethics cases using structured ethical reasoning. Each case must address a different domain: (1) patient data privacy, (2) AI/algorithmic ethics, (3) health equity and access. Include ethical analysis, stakeholder perspectives, and recommendations.
objectives: Apply ethical frameworks to real-world health informatics dilemmas
Analyze cases from multiple stakeholder perspectives
Practice structured ethical reasoning and communication
deliverables: Three case analyses (4-5 pages each) using the four-principles framework
Stakeholder analysis for each case identifying competing interests
Reasoned ethical recommendations for each case
Reflective essay (3 pages) on personal ethical development
Presentation of one case to the class (12 minutes)

criteria: All four principles correctly applied to each case
Grounded in ethical reasoning, not just opinion
Honest personal reflection on ethical development
Ability to respond to ethical challenges from peers
Rigorous ethical analysis using established frameworks
Thoughtful stakeholder analysis with diverse perspectives
Actionable recommendations grounded in ethical reasoning
Genuine personal reflection and professional presentation

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of ethical concepts, frameworks, and case analysis skills

class_participation.description: Active engagement in ethics discussions, debates, and case analyses

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of ethical principles and privacy ethics

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including AI ethics, equity, and professional ethics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is essential for ethics courses that depend on discussion and debate. More than 3 unexcused absences reduce the participation grade to zero.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late case analyses lose 10% per day. Ethics is a time-sensitive discipline; late analysis misses the class discussion context.
course_policies.collaboration: Ethics discussions are inherently collaborative. Written analyses must be individual work reflecting the student's own ethical reasoning.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Ethics case analyses must demonstrate original reasoning. Copying ethical arguments without attribution is particularly ironic in an ethics course and will result in a zero.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Internet access for case research. No specialized software required.
